frameworks.name: hemodynamic response function (HRF) modeling
frameworks.one_line: Cell-type-specific HRFs link neuronal activity to BOLD signals.

frameworks.name: neurovascular coupling
frameworks.one_line: Excitatory and inhibitory activity differentially drive BOLD responses.

frameworks.name: optogenetic cell-type dissection
frameworks.one_line: VGAT-ChR2 and CaMKII-ChR2 separate inhibitory and excitatory contributions.

core_question: How do excitatory versus inhibitory neuronal activities each contribute to the BOLD fMRI signal?
key_assumptions: BOLD signals can be decomposed into cell-type-specific neural contributions
Optogenetic stimulation in VGAT/CaMKII mice selectively drives inhibitory or excitatory populations
Hemodynamic response can be modeled as a linear function of neuronal activity
manipulations: Cell type targeted (VGAT-ChR2 inhibitory vs CaMKII-ChR2 excitatory)
key_findings: Excitatory and somatosensory stimulation produced positive BOLD responses in the somatosensory network
Inhibitory stimulation produced biphasic BOLD (initial positive, later negative) at the site and negative BOLD downstream
Shortening inhibitory stimulation to 5 s yielded only positive BOLD responses
Inhibitory HRF is highly sensitive to stimulation frequency and peaks earlier than excitatory HRF
interpretations: Inhibitory neurons contribute distinctly and nonlinearly to BOLD, with cell-type-specific HRFs
Excitatory HRF is comparatively stable across conditions while inhibitory HRF is condition-dependent
